MOTION / Notice Of Motion & Motion To Modify The Designation Of Record By Plaintiff; Memorandum Of Points & Authorities; Declaration Of Counsel
Off Calendar Oct-25-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances via Zoom:
Becky S. James, Esq. of James & Associates, 310.492.5104, for plaintiff Maher Memarzadeh;
Valeria Granata, Esq. of Wilson, Elser, Moskowitz, Edelman & Dicker LLP, 213.443.5100, for defendant Francisco Arturo Gutierrez.

Hearing re: DEFENDANT FRANCISCO GUTIERREZ'S Motion To Modify The Designation Of Record By Plaintiff.

This matter is contested. Argument is held. At the conclusion of argument, the Court adopted the tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Francisco Guiterrez's motion to modify the designation of record by plaintiff is denied.

Plaintiff Maher Memarzadeh is appealing the final judgment of this legal malpractice action, after the court imposed terminating sanctions against him. Guiterrez now moves under California Rules of Court, Rule 8.124, for an order requiring use of a clerk's transcript on appeal, in lieu of the appendix that Memarzadeh selected.

Rule 8.124(a)(1)(B) allows a party to challenge an appellant's election to use an appendix "within 10 days after the appellant's notice designating the record on appeal is filed." Here, Memarzadeh filed his "notice designating record on appeal," on August 8, 2024, and served the notice by e-mail the same day. Thus, Guiterrez's last day to file and serve this motion was Monday, August 19, 2024. Even assuming the e-service statute applies and extends this window by two court days, Guiterrez's last day to file would have been August 21, 2024. (See CCP 1010.6(a)(3)(B).) As Guiterrez filed this motion on September 6, 2024, it is untimely.

Guiterrez argues his motion is timely because he served it on August 15, 2024. (Granata decl. Ex. A.) But Rule 8.124 requires the motion be "served and filed within 10 days," not just served. (Subd. (a)(1).) Further, the rule leaves no discretion for the court to consider an untimely motion, even if the motion was rejected and did not change in substance once it was re-filed. (See Reply 3:3-10.) Gutierrez cites no other authority that grants such discretion, so the motion must be denied.

Court to review the proposed order submitted by the prevailing party.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Court Reporter: Janice Hong, CSR #11975, Tel. 909.831.2644, Email: csrjane@hotmail.com. Bailiff: Petrucci. (301/RCE)

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Or Tax Costs; Memorandum Of Points And Authorities; Declaration Of Becky S. James; Exhibit
Off Calendar Oct-02-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

APPEARANCES (VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE):
Atty. BECKY S. JAMES, Esq. of James & Associates (Tel: 310-492-5104) for plaintiff;
Atty. VALERIA GRANATA, Esq. of Wilson, Elser, Moskowitz, Edelman & Dicker LLP (Tel: 213-443-5100) for defendant Francisco Arturo Gutierrez.

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF MAHER MEMARZADEH's Motion To Strike Or Tax Costs.

Matter argued and submitted. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

After prevailing in this action, defendant seeks costs of $40,399. Plaintiff moved "to strike or tax costs." That motion is adjudged as follows.

Expert Witness Fees. Plaintiff did not accept defendant's $40,000 CCP 998 offer and thus became liable for defendant's expert witness fees from the time of the offer. (Id. at (c)(1).) Having ruled on many motions in this case, the court finds $40,000 was not a "token," as plaintiff asserts. Moreover, CCP 998(c)(1) authorizes expert witness fees "in preparation for trial or arbitration," not just for testimony. (Michelson v. Camp (1999) 72 Cal.App.4th 955, 975.) $21,344 is recoverable.

Service of Deposition Subpoenas. These costs are recoverable in the amount of $3,180 (CCP 1033.5(a)(4)) and supported by invoices. (Granata Dec. Ex. 3.)

Filing and Motion Fees. These costs are recoverable in the amount of $2,180.

Sanctions/"Other" Costs. The February 22, 2024 order granting sanctions is already in the court record; now also ordering these sanctions as costs would invite confusion. Further, the court exercises its discretion to strike the "other" costs as not adequately supported. $9,848 is taxed.

Reporter Fees. Deposition costs are recoverable, including transcription costs, in the amount of $3,847. (CCP 1033.5(a)(3)(A).)

In sum, defendant is awarded $30,551 in costs.

Order signed.

Judge: Richard B. Ulmer Jr.; Clerk: S. Kane; Court Reporter: Raquel Sharp, CSR No. 10619, Email: raquel@transcriptlegal.com, Tel: 925-226-8222 (302/RBU)

MINI MINUTES:
APPEARANCES VIA ZOOM VIDEOCONFERENCE:
Morris Getzels, Esq. (818.881.1176; morris@getzelslaw.com) for Plaintiff.
Valeria Granata, Esq. (213.330.8927; valeria.granata@wilsonelser.com) of Wilson Elser Moskowitz Edelman & Dicker LLP for Defendant.

DEFENDANT FRANCISCO GUTIERREZ's DEMURRER to 1ST Amended COMPLAINT

Defendant's demurrer to the first amended complaint (FAC) is overruled in part and sustained in part.

First, fraud damage is adequately pled. The FAC can be fairly read to allege that plaintiff continued to pay legal fees after plaintiff's alleged false statements and concealments regarding the statute of limitations. (Id. at 5:1-4, 5:28-6:4.) This part of the demurrer is overruled.

Second, the "breach of fiduciary duty" claim is based on the notion that defendant should have sued engineering consultants retained by an insurer. This sounds in negligence, not fiduciary duty; plaintiff cites no contrary authority. This part of the demurrer is sustained without leave to amend.

Order is signed.
